titlestmt.titleproper: Guide to the Robert Ashe Papers
titlestmt.author: Quin DeLaRosa

publicationstmt.publisher: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

controlnote.p: This encoded finding aid is compliant with Yale EAD Best Practice Guidelines, Version 1.0.

maintenanceagency.agencyname: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

This means that anyone may copy, modify, and distribute this descriptive metadata without restriction or permission. The CC0 license is explained in full on the Creative Commons website: CC0 1.0 Universal (CC0 1.0) Public Domain Dedication. More information is available on our Help page.

maintenanceevent.eventdescription: This finding aid was produced using ArchivesSpace on Monday April 6, 2026 at 23:02

corpname.part: Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library

descriptivenote.p: Chiefly in English, some material in French and Khmer.

did.abstract: The Robert Ashe papers consist of correspondence, reports, photographs, press clippings, publications, and maps documenting British humanitarian worker Robert Ashe's involvement with international relief efforts to aid Cambodian refugees in the late 1970s and early 1980s.

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Organized into four series: I. Correspondence and reports, 1977-1984; II. Photographs, circa 1975-1981; III. Press and publications, 1974-1986; IV. Maps, circa 1953-1970.

bioghist.head: Biographical / Historical
bioghist.p: Robert Ashe (1953-2022) was an English humanitarian best known for his role as director of Nong Chan refugee camp on the Thai/Cambodian border and later career with the United Nations High Commissioner for Refugees (UNHCR).

Born in Woolwich, England, Ashe completed his studies at the Agricultural College in Cirencester before embarking in 1973 on a series of humanitarian posts in Southeast Asia. His work with refugees began in Vietnam as a member of Project Vietnam Orphans (PVO), a non-profit organization that his parents founded to support orphaned and abandoned children. This association with PVO – since renamed to Christian Outreach – evolved into a sustained leadership role from 1975-1980, during which time Ashe directed other international volunteers in providing aid within refugee camps along the Thai/Cambodian border. From 1979-1980, while seconded to the International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC), Ashe directed the operations of the Nong Chan refugee camp on the border between Aranya Prathet in Thailand and Poipet in Cambodia. Ashe implemented the "Land Bridge" initiative, a cross-border operation to funnel food and agricultural tools to refugees in Cambodia via Thailand. It was during this time Ashe became embroiled in an international incident as one of four Western civilians taken hostage by Vietnamese troops from June 27-30, 1980, amidst their military offensive into Thailand.

Following his work with ICRC, Ashe worked with Food for the Hungry International (FHI) before joining UNHCR for the remainder of his humanitarian career. UNHRC assigned Ashe to posts in Sudan (1986-1989); Geneva, Switzerland (1989-1994, 1996-2002); The Hague, Netherlands (1995-1996); and Jakarta, Indonesia (2002-2009).
Ashe was married to Var Hong, a Cambodian refugee at the time they met, from 1982-1993. He later married Aam Zamila Dachlan in 2002, whom he remained with until his death in Indonesia in 2022.

accessrestrict.head: Conditions Governing Access
accessrestrict.p: This collection is open for research.

userestrict.head: Conditions Governing Use
userestrict.p: Copyright and other associated proprietary rights for unpublished materials authored or otherwise produced by Robert Ashe was transferred to Yale University in 2023. These materials may be used for non-commercial purposes without seeking permission from Yale University as the copyright holder. For other uses of these materials, please contact beinecke.library@yale.edu. Copyright status for other collection materials is unknown. Transmission or reproduction of materials protected by U.S. Copyright Law (Title 17, U.S.C.) beyond that allowed by fair use requires the written permission of the copyright owners. Works not in the public domain cannot be commercially exploited without permission of the copyright owners. Responsibility for any use rests exclusively with the user.

prefercite.head: Preferred Citation
prefercite.p: Robert Ashe Papers. General Collection, Beinecke Rare Book and Manuscript Library, Yale University.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Collections are processed to a variety of levels, depending on the work necessary to make them usable, their perceived research value, the availability of staff, competing priorities, and whether or not further accruals are expected. The library attempts to provide a basic level of preservation and access for all collections, and does more extensive processing of higher priority collections as time and resources permit.
These materials have been arranged and described according to national and local standards. For more information, please refer to the Yale Archival Processing Manual.

The order of the materials has been substantially rearranged by the archivist to promote usability. Creator-assigned descriptive information has been used to devise file titles and can be identified by the use of quotation marks.
A selection of the materials in Series IV are currently undergoing in-house conservation and will be made available for research following the completion of this intervention.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The Robert Ashe papers consist of correspondence, reports, photographs, press clippings, publications, and maps documenting British humanitarian worker Robert Ashe's involvement with international relief efforts to aid Cambodian refugees in the late 1970s and early 1980s.

The collection provides a window into Ashe's experience directing a refugee camp amidst a complex political situation and in coordination with several international humanitarian organizations. Much of the materials also document the experiences of Cambodians fleeing political violence, famine, and economic devastation in the aftermath of the Cambodian Genocide and subsequent Vietnamese occupation of Cambodia.

Correspondence, reports, and photographs illustrate Ashe's personal experiences and reflections on his humanitarian work along the Thai/Cambodian border. Maps, press clippings, and publications collected by Ashe provide a wider perspective on the refugee crisis extending potentially as early as the 1950s and as late as the mid-1980s. While English is the predominant language of the materials, numerous instances of French and Khmer are present.
Sensitive content is present in Boxes 3 and 5, which contain photographs featuring graphic depictions of deceased persons.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series I contains correspondence and reports created, received, and/or compiled by Robert Ashe.

Correspondents include numerous private individuals, many of whom sought Ashe's perspective for their research on humanitarian operations. Representatives of numerous organizations -- government, international, and non-governmental -- are represented in the correspondence as well. This notably includes the militant partisan leader Kong Sileah. Ashe's reports to ICRC and Christian Outreach, circa 1979-1981, feature heavily in this series. Ashe's leadership of Nong Chan, especially regarding the operations of the "Land Bridge," is a central topic across each of these categories.
Personal narratives written by Cambodian refugees are interspersed across this series. Most of these writings have been translated into English.

did.unittitle: Refugee narratives and correspondence with British officials
Robert Ashe, The Dark Night pamphlet
Correspondence, reports, press, and personal narratives on the forced repatriation of Cambodian refugees

descriptivenote.p: Material is in English and Khmer.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also includes an article by historian Ben Kiernan.

did.unittitle: Refugee narratives and Paul Quinn-Judge's Notes on the Political Sources of Conflict Between Vietnam and Cambodia

descriptivenote.p: Material is in English and Khmer.

did.unittitle: Correspondence between forcibly repatriated refugees and their friends and families
Reports on Nong Chan Land Bridge, speech by Tith Sarun to UN Secretary-General Waldheim, and refugee narrative
Reports to Christian Outreach and other correspondence
Robert Ashe's correspondence with Kong Sileah and the Moulinaka

descriptivenote.p: Material is in English and French.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also includes some clippings relating to the Khmer Serei groups and a few photocopied maps.

did.unittitle: Robert Ashe's reports to ICRC and related correspondence

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Also includes a few hand-drawn maps and one ICRC publication.

did.unittitle: Correspondence and reports involving ICRC and Christian Outreach
Reports and clippings on famine and food distribution
Correspondence on Robert Ashe's capture by Vietnamese military forces
Correspondence on the operations of Nong Chan refugee camp
Correspondence and reports from the Comité Européen d'Aide aux Réfugiés
Draft and correspondence relating to book chapter
Robert Ashe's correspondence with William Shawcross and Robert Porter on book production
Correspondence on international relief aid and related television program
Descriptions of the Cambodian Genocide by the Tuol Sleng Museum of Genocidal Crime
Personal narrative on the Nong Chan refugee camp

arrangement.head: Arrangement
arrangement.p: Series II is arranged primarily by ascending numerical groupings of film rolls. Within film rolls, files are arranged chronologically.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: Photographs were removed from their original housing -- small, flip photo albums -- for preservation considerations during processing. These albums have been retained for their inscriptions and production information and are represented in the description by the file "Original photographic housing, circa 1976-1981."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series II contains approximately 1,100 color and black-and-white photographic prints compiled by Robert Ashe. With the exception of one file containing 8"x10" prints, almost all are roughly 3"x5". Versos often contain additional information such as film roll numbers and, ocassionally, descriptive captions.

Image content features extensive subject matter relating to life in the refugee camps with a focus on Nong Chan. Such content includes though is not limited to images depicting Robert Ashe, resident and non-resident refugees, relief workers, construction of camp facilities, recreational activities, childrens' educational programming, distribution of rice by ICRC, distribution of fishing nets by Christian Outreach, and the funeral of militant partisan leader Kong Sileah.
Graphic depictions of deceased persons are present in Boxes 3 and 5. These images include depictions of mass graves, decomposing corpses, and victims of the Cambodian Genocide.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographic content includes graphic depictions of deceased persons.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographic content includes graphic depictions of deceased persons.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographic content includes graphic depictions of deceased persons.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographic content includes graphic depictions of deceased persons.

did.unittitle: Mass graves and victims of the Cambodian Genocide

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains 8 8"x10" black-and-white photographs. Photographic content includes graphic depictions of deceased persons.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographic content includes graphic depictions of deceased persons.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: An inscription on the original housing suggests these images could potentially comprise parts of film roll 36, yet the dates cast doubt on this.

did.unittitle: "April 1979 Wat Koh, Aran. New arrivals (taken by Buzz)"

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: The following statement is inscribed on the original photograph housing: "Being done in conjunction with a rice distribution which is not funded or organised by C.O.R. Nets are in packets of 10 on top of rice sacks. Each group of 30 people people receive 2 packets of nets."

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains images from film rolls C, F, and possibly more.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Contains images from film rolls A, B, C, D, and possibly more.

did.unittitle: Photogaphy book by Tim Carney, Kampuchea: Balance of Survival

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Photographs taken from 1979-1980. Book published in 1981.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series III contains press clippings and publications collected by Robert Ashe during his humanitarian posts in Mainland Southeast Asia. These works are of varying national, international, and organizational origins. Collectively, they highlight the evolving political turmoil in the region between the 1970s and 1980s, spotlighting its impacts on the Cambodian refugee crisis and accompanying international humanitarian response.

Most materials in this series represent the following national or international perspectives and publications: British ( The Guardian , Observer ); American ( Newsweek , Time , The New Yorker ); English-language Hong Kong ( Far Eastern Economic Review ); English-language Thai ( Bangkok Post , Voice of the Nation / The Nation , Bangkok World , Business in Thailand ); English-language publications from non-governmental organizations (i.e. from the International Committee of the Red Cross); and limited instances of unidentified publications. The most abundant viewpoint is that of the English-language, Thai print news media.

did.unittitle: ICRC, Red Cross Times Special Edition: Report from the ICRC delegation in Thailand
Thailand Ministry of Interior, An Instrument of Foreign Policy: Indochinese Displaced Persons
ICRC, Kampuchea: Back From The Brink
Articles from The New Yorker and The New Republic

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Series IV contains numerous maps collected by Robert Ashe.

Most of the materials in this series are published, large-format maps. These feature political and topographical projections of Cambodia, including its capital of Phnom Penh, in English, French, and Khmer. Also included is a small quantity of hand-drawn sketch maps depicting the locations of refugee camps and other notable sites along the Thai/Cambodian border.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: A selection of the materials in Series IV are currently undergoing in-house conservation and will be made available for research following the completion of this intervention. At present, these materials are represented by the file "Miscellaneous maps, undated."

did.unittitle: French map of the Kingdom of Cambodia
U.S. Army map of Phnom Penh

descriptivenote.p: Material is in English, Khmer, and French.

did.unittitle: Hand-drawn sketch map of Nong Chan refugee camp
Hand-drawn sketch map of Thai/Cambodian border refugee camps and road system

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: These materials, comprising a minimum of six items, are currently undergoing in-house conservation to support long-term preservation and access. The items will receive enhanced descriptions of their content and be made available for research following the completion of this intervention. Please contact the appropriate curator for further information.
